title: 08 FourierSeries 4pp
body:
Aims MATH2019 – Engineering Mathematics 2E Topic 8: Fourier Series I Many applications in engineering involve phenomena that have a cyclical or periodic behaviour. For example, the motion of a piston or the oscillation of an audio speaker. Dr Bill Ellis I You know that the sine and cosine functions are periodic. School of Mathematics and Statistics The University of New South Wales Fourier series provide us with a tool to analyse periodic Sydney, Australia phenomena by transforming periodic signals into a series Email: wjellis@unsw.edu.au (or infinite sum) of sines and cosines. I In this topic, you will learn how to represent a periodic function by its Fourier series. You will also learn how to solve the problem of forced oscillations (see Topic 5) using Fourier series. March 31, 2022 . .

What you need to know What you will learn

I Calculate the Fourier series of a periodic function. I Extend a function given on the interval [0, L] (for some L > 0) to a periodic function defined on the whole real In this topic, periodic functions, as well as even and odd line. functions, are of great importance. You have learnt about such I Calculate the half-range Fourier expansion of a function functions in high school and/or first year. The first section given on the interval [0, L]. contains a summary of key facts. I Use Fourier series to solve a nonhomogeneous second order linear ODE when the right-hand side is a periodic function. I Apply this technique to solve the problem of forced oscillations.

. .  Outline Periodic functions

Periodic Functions Definition 1 A function f is said to be periodic when there exists T > 0 Fourier Series such that if f is defined at x then it is also defined at x + T and Fourier Series with Arbitrary Period f (x + T ) = f (x). The smallest positive T satisfying the above identity is called Periodic Extensions the period of f . Note Half-Range Expansions The function f may be undefined at a number of points. If f is undefined at x0 , then f is also undefined at x0 ± T , Forced Oscillations and Fourier Series x0 ± 2T , ….

. .

Periodic functions Example 2 I sin x and cos x are periodic with period 2π. I tan x is periodic with period π, and is undefined at ±π/2, ±3π/2, ….

Example 3 Show that the periods of sin(nx) and cos(nx) are T = 2π/n. x − 2T x−T x x+T Proof. Let f (x) = sin(nx). Then

f (x + T ) = sin[n(x + T )] = sin(nx + nT )

equals f (x) = sin nx for all x if and only nT = 2π, because sin x has period 2π. Similarly for cos(nx). Note n is called the wave number, i.e., the number of waves in . . 2π. . .  Even and odd functions Symmetry of even and odd functions

I The graph of an even function is symmetric about the y-axis. Definition 6 I The graph of an odd function is symmetric about the I A function f is said to be even if origin of the axes. 1. whenever f is defined at x, it is also defined at −x and 2. f (−x) = f (x).

I A function f is said to be odd if f (x) 1. whenever f is defined at x, it is also defined at −x and f (−x) = f (x) −x 2. f (−x) = −f (x). x f (−x) −x x

. .

Why ‘even’ and ‘odd’? Example 7 For integers p and q: Which of the following functions is odd, even, or neither? 1. f (x) = cos(3x); p q p+q 2. f (x) = x cos(x); odd odd even odd even odd 3. f (x) = sin2 (x). even odd odd even even even
